title: Context-sensitive keywords should be avoided as identifiers
description: Context-sensitive keywords should not be used as identifiersAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmellpitfallWhy is this an issue?More InfoThe C++ standards define some identifiers as having special meaning in specific contexts. These are:finalandoverridesince C++11moduleandimportsince C++20While it is technically possible to use them as normal identifiers, it’s clearer for the reader of the code to consider them as if they were keywords and only use them with their special meaning.Noncompliant code examplevoid module(int final); // NoncompliantCompliant solutionvoid precept(int finalValue); // CompliantAvailable In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise C
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: void module(int final);  // Noncompliant: 'module' and 'final' used as identifiers
int override;        // Noncompliant: 'override' used as variable name
void import();       // Noncompliant: 'import' used as function name
compliant: // Using proper identifiers instead of context-sensitive keywords
void precept(int finalValue);  // Compliant: clear identifier names
int overrideValue;         // Compliant: descriptive variable name
void importData();         // Compliant: clear function name
// Using context-sensitive keywords in their proper context
class Derived final : public Base {  // 'final' used correctly

checks.message: Avoid using context-sensitive keywords as identifiers.
